items.myth: Decision trees always outperform linear models on tabular data.
items.reality: Trees excel when boundaries are nonlinear and local, but on linearly separable or very small data a regularized logistic or linear model can generalize better with lower variance.
items.example: Breast cancer Wisconsin (mostly linear structure): shallow logistic regression sometimes matches or beats an untuned deep tree on CV accuracy.

items.myth: Gini and entropy produce fundamentally different trees.
items.reality: Both measure class mixing in a node; they usually agree on the best split. Differences appear at ties or borderline gains, not as a wholesale change in model family.
items.example: sklearn criterion='gini' vs 'entropy' on iris with max_depth=3 often yields identical structure and accuracy.

items.myth: Deeper trees are always better because they fit training data more closely.
items.reality: Extra depth lowers training error but raises variance — validation error often increases after a point. Complexity must match dataset size and noise.
items.example: Validation accuracy vs max_depth curve peaks at depth 4–6 then drops as depth → 20 on a 500-sample set.

items.myth: A decision tree learns a globally optimal partition of feature space.
items.reality: CART is greedy: each split is locally optimal given current nodes. Early suboptimal splits cannot be undone — unlike global methods that optimize all parameters jointly.
items.example: A feature useful only in combination with another may be ignored at the root because a greedy single-feature split looks weak alone.

items.myth: Feature importance from a single tree is a reliable causal ranking.
items.reality: Importance reflects split usage and impurity reduction in this specific high-variance model. Correlated features share credit; causal interpretation requires domain care and stable ensembles.
items.example: zip_code and latitude may both rank high when correlated — neither alone proves causation of the target.
